pi_items.source: setup_task.sh line 14-15 copies data/oauth_dfd.drawio to this path; export_result.sh line 23 references this path
export_result.sh line 24 defines SVG_PATH; verifier.py line 177-184 checks for SVG at this location
export_result.sh line 25 defines PDF_PATH; verifier.py line 186-194 checks for PDF at this location
Direct XML analysis of oauth_dfd.drawio shows exactly 1 <diagram> element with name='OAuth 2.0 DFD'; setup_task.sh line 40 records initial_page_count as '1'

task.json description: 'creating a second diagram page named Threat Enumeration'; README.md success criteria: 'Second page (Threat Enumeration) created' worth 15 points; stride_reference.txt line 64: 'Create a second diagram page named "Threat Enumeration"'
stride_reference.txt line 67: 'Minimum 8 threat entries required'; task.json description: 'containing a threat table with at least 8 rows'

stride_reference.txt defines STRIDE methodology with exactly 6 categories: S-Spoofing (line 9), T-Tampering (line 15), R-Repudiation (line 21), I-Information Disclosure (line 27), D-Denial of Service (line 33), E-Elevation of Privilege (line 39). This is the standard Microsoft STRIDE methodology.

privileged_info_summary: The oauth_threat_model.drawio is a synthetically created example diagram representing an OAuth 2.0 Data Flow Diagram with 2 external entities, 2 processes, 3 data stores, and 10 labeled data flows. The initial file contains exactly 1 diagram page. The STRIDE methodology reference material defines the standard 6 threat categories (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) and requires creation of a second threat enumeration page with at least 8 threat entries. Export outputs are expected at /home/ga/Diagrams/oauth_threat_model.svg (DFD page) and /home/ga/Diagrams/oauth_threat_model.pdf (all pages).
